detect.predict.instructions: Decide whether the supplied passage was generated by an AI.
detect.predict.signature:
StringSignature(text -> reasoning, is_ai
    instructions='Decide whether the supplied passage was generated by an AI.'
    text = Field(annotation=str required=True json_schema_extra={'desc': 'Passage to classify', '__dspy_field_type': 'input', 'prefix': 'Text:'})
    reasoning = Field(annotation=str required=True json_schema_extra={'desc': '${reasoning}', '__dspy_field_type': 'output', 'prefix': 'Reasoning:'})
    is_ai = Field(annotation=bool required=True json_schema_extra={'desc': 'True for AI-generated text; otherwise False', '__dspy_field_type': 'output', 'prefix': 'Is Ai:'})
)

fields.name: is_ai
fields.description: True for AI-generated text; otherwise False

demos.text: In general, we advise you to try and keep the topology (the layout) of your DAG tasks relatively stable; dynamic DAGs are usually better used for dynamically loading configuration options or changing operator options.
demos.reasoning: The passage has the concise, prescriptive style of technical documentation and uses domain-specific Airflow terminology (“DAG tasks,” “dynamic DAGs,” and “operator options”) in a natural way. Its wording appears like a human-authored documentation guideline rather than distinctly AI-generated prose.

demos.text: In general, try to keep the topology (that is, the layout) of your DAG tasks reasonably stable; dynamic DAGs tend to work better for loading configuration values dynamically or adjusting operator options as needed.
demos.reasoning: The passage uses polished technical language and a generalized instructional tone, with a somewhat awkward contrast between keeping DAG topology stable and using dynamic DAGs. This phrasing is characteristic of AI-generated explanatory text.
